speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're--

speaker: interviewer
text: Date May 24, 1995. The survivor's name is Nicholas Gardos. And his former name was Weisz. The interviewer is Esthes Stearnes in Sydney, New South Wales. And the country is Australia. The language is English. I am Esthes Stearnes, and I am conducting an interview with Mr. Nicholas Gardos in Sydney, Australia. It is May the 24th, PM, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Esthes Stearnes interviewing Mr. Nicholas Gardos in Sydney, Australia on Wednesday May the 24th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us your full name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nicholas Gardos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your given name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Miklos. ?] Should I spell it for you?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And surname at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: And surname Weisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Moshe ?] [? Mordechai ?] ben Asher [? Miklos. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 3rd of the 7th, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a place called Nogradveroce, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far would that be from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 85 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you-- your family moved from the place where you were born to another town. Could you tell us where they moved to and when that happened, how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was four years old. My grandparents in-- [? lived ?] the place where I was born, in Nogradveroce. They had a general-- was a general food shop. And every village had a food shop like that. And in most circumstances, most of the time, the owners of the shoppers were our people. So this was one of the-- one of the religious and one of the shops which was owned by my grandparents. My father worked for a number of years, about eight years, nine years, in the shop. And then we moved up to Ujpest, which is a suburb of Budapest, is 10 kilometers from the-- from the center of the city. The reason why we moved up was mainly because they established a-- a business in conjunction with my-- with my-- grand-- other grandfather from my mother's side and his two sons, my uncles. They established a-- the business was established, actually, because it was a factory which was running already for 10, 15 years. But it didn't have-- didn't have a wholesale outlet, which my father took on. And from then on, he was the sole outlet of that-- of that business, of that factory. Just for interest's sake, that place where I was born, in that village, I was born in the same house as my father was born. So I have some pictures for you after if you're interested in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your other family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: The closest family members?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the two sets of grandparents, my grandmother and grandfather from my-- from my mother's side, and-- and also the same from my father's side. My father's only brother died or fell-- fell in the First World-- First World War on the Italian front. And my mother's two brothers, they both perished during the Holocaust. One-- one died in Auschwitz as one of the last people who dismantled the-- one of the factories in Auschwitz. And it was too late to evacuate him. And the Germans shot him, one of the last days before the Russians came. And my other uncle, my mother's brother, was shot somewhere near Vienna on a march from [INAUDIBLE], from Yugoslavia, to somewhere, this unknown destination, in Austria. He was one of the people who-- who couldn't walk any longer. And he was one of those-- one of many thousands of people who was shot because he couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- my other immediate family-- family, of course my sister. She was older?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was six years older, who came here to Australia with her husband and children in 1956. And she just died in 1993 at the age if 70. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father died in-- in Muldorf. We were together in Muldorf, in the camp. And he died on the 29th of December, 1944, which happens to be my mother's birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go back to your early family life. I believe that you spoke Yiddish and that you were a fairly religious family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you  like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, had to tell you how religious. Religious, naturally that any-- any-- anybody who says they're religious starts from-- from the-- from home. Of course kosher, it was-- it was nothing. There wasn't anything extra religious to have a kosher household. But on the other hand, none of my family has-- was one of those ultra-Orthodox people who had a beard. Nobody had a beard in the family. At the same time, everybody observed the Shabbat. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it would have been-- I think it was, to my knowledge, nobody ever ate, even after the [INAUDIBLE], a trace of food. No need to say that we observed all the holidays. And in Hungary, there were two types of people-- actually three, but the third one was very much a minority. One was the so-called-- I'm talking about Jewish people. One was so-called [NON-ENGLISH], which is-- it's not a Reform, because at that time, there was no-- no such a thing as Reform. Not in Hungary anyway, there was in Germany. They were-- they didn't observe the Shabbat, but at the same time they observed all the holidays. More or less they kept kosher, but-- but not so 100%. And-- and they were, like everybody else, they were more Jews of convenience. What was convenient for them, that was the same as Jews they were. Then-- then came our section which was more observant. And the children were brought up [? like ?] myself. I had my education in the-- in the Jewish gymnasium, which is equal to Jewish high school like [? Moriah ?] here. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and naturally, we put far more importance on religion and being Jewish than the one-- I wouldn't say-- I wouldn't use the word below us, because other people would take offense-- but not next to us. Then we had a very-- we had also people with-- very religious people, ultrareligious people. There was [NON-ENGLISH] and beard and [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a yeshiva in-- in Budapest, who were not-- not close to us, not because we didn't respect them by any means, but because they had a different lifestyle. Like they have a different lifestyle here. Which again, here it doesn't mean that-- I'm very close-- I'm very close to the issue. I'm very close Rabbi [? Feldman. ?] And I support the yeshiva as much as I can because I realize the work they're doing. So-- so much for a-- If that is-- if that is a question you asked, I hope-- I hope I gave you some sort of information which would be satisfactory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What synagogue-- what was the name of the synagogue that your family attended in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it is different-- synagogues, most of the synagogues in Hungary, because there were not so many of them like here, didn't have specific names. In the Western world, every synagogue has got a name. There's a temple or a synagogue whatever, it's got a name. In Hungary, there was-- there was a township. And it was the synagogue of that particular township. In Budapest, yes, there was. For example, you take the biggest synagogue in Hungary, and probably in Europe, which is called the Dohany utcai, which is today-- I believe everybody's flocking there to see. It was the most beautiful synagogue. But our synagogue was about half an hour walk away, which was called the [INAUDIBLE] synagogue. It's a hard word to-- it's a hard word to understand, but whoever would listen to it ever, to our interview, here to this film, and will hear the word, will also know exactly what-- which synagogue I'm referring to. It was a big synagogue. It seated about 1,500 to 1,800 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe in your primary school years you went to the public school in Ujpest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you be able to describe any episodes of anti-Semitism that occurred at that school or going to and from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, to be quite specific, as far as my schooling was concerned, the first-- my first year of schooling, I went to cheder. But for some reason or another I didn't-- I didn't sort of didn't fit into the cheder. And therefore, we-- my parents choose-- have chosen to put me into public school for-- for primary. Yeah, in primary school, we-- it was-- it was most common. And I don't think that there was nothing personal about it, because I think it was aimed at every Jewish student, that they called us-- called us dirty Jews and stinky Jews. And-- and it-- this-- this was the classmates, and not everyone of them. A lot of them were very good friends. At the same time, the teachers certainly did-- did not discourage them to-- to upset us or to insult us. If there was such a-- such a teacher, well, I think he kept very much to himself and didn't show it in the public because he wouldn't have been very popular. But at that-- at that time, which was 1935, 1936, there was no real-- no real hateful anti-Semitism in-- in Hungary. And there was not such a thing as anybody being hurt bodily in a school, no more than a usual fistfight or something between boys, which happens in any school. Doesn't matter what-- what public school, private school. I don't think there was-- who-- I was-- it would be more exaggerated if I would say it was a-- it-- it wasn't anything serious, put it this way. Wasn't anything serious. Not in 1936, '37, certainly not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were playing soccer in a team. And was that local Hungarian boys who were Jewish were playing against you or with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we play-- we play soccer in-- in teams against other schools, and like the same is-- the same is here. I don't know if you ever went to a soccer match here. But whenever there's a-- there was [INAUDIBLE] playing, even the players who were not Jewish, they were called dirty Jews. And-- and other teams were running them down and so forth. It was no different there. There-- there was nothing exceptional to see stickers out on-- on-- on lampposts, 'Jews get out,' sort of thing. That-- that was something very-- something which we were used to. Nobody-- nobody worried about it because it was just a way of life, at-- that-- as far as primary school was concerned. As time progressed, and I was about 10 years old, 1939-- when-- when-- 1939, that's right, when I started high school. That already started to be a bit nastier. But it still was nothing-- nothing which anybody would say that the writing was on the wall. There was not such a thing at that time because up to 1942, Jews were part of the regular army. And even Jews were officers of the regular army. So 1942 was the turning point, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Jewish gymnasium that you went to for your high school years, would you say that your other Jewish classmates were fairly assimilated, or were some of them quite religious like the group you referred to before? What sort of groupings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most of them were-- were assimilated, year.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't speaking Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no-- there were no such as a thing as students with [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't have anybody. I can't remember ever seeing anybody. It was-- it was like Moriah, very much like Moriah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had two-- actually it had two-- it had two different-- the school was-- one school was for general studies, like a high school. And the other school was called technical school, a technical-- technical high school. And a technical high school has-- was something like here. The-- what do you call it? Tech. Technical high school was, on a smaller scale, was the tech here, except under-- under the-- under the umbrella of the Jewish gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- I was attending the regular-- regular high school. I have one person here who also attended the same school. But he was in the other part, in the-- in the technical. As a matter of fact, there were more people who went to school together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- your Jewish classmates would speak together in Hungarian and perhaps speak some Yiddish at home in some cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I wouldn't know of anybody who spoke Yiddish at home. We didn't speak Yiddish at home. Nor did-- nor do I know of anybody who spoke Yiddish at home. Because my-- my grandparent-- my grandparents from my father's side, they spoke Yiddish at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you understood the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: I understood-- I-- yeah, I understood it. And later on, circumstances made me remember it and learn it very quickly again. But from my mother's side, nobody spoke Yiddish. Also religious, also Orthodox, but nobody spoke Yiddish. And neither did anybody to my knowledge in the high school, in the gymnasium. Yiddish was-- was more confined, as I said to you, to the smallest section of very, very ultra-Orthodox Jews, who-- who they-- they didn't have-- they had-- they had a yeshiva, a very big yeshiva. And then, of course, they spoke nothing us but Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just give us a general and brief description of the relationships between the Jews and the non-Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I have to go back-- I have to go back a bit in time. Up to up to 1940, 1941, the relationship was most amicable. And there was strong friendship between Jews and non-Jews in-- in Budapest. Now, I'll say Budapest because Ujpest and also other suburbs were-- were like-- like great-- great Budapest, great cities. Like we're talking about 10 kilometers [INAUDIBLE] in the city, [INAUDIBLE] distance. And the relationship was-- was I would say, extremely good, except-- except, of course, when it came to politics. When it came to politics, we had the-- we had the party, the Nazi party in-- in Hungary, like everywhere else. And they make no bones about it that they wanted-- what-- what-- what their aim was. They didn't say they wanted to-- wanted to kill the Jews, but they wanted to get rid of the Jews. Whatever there was wrong in the country, whatever-- whatever there was any-- any misfortune, or-- everything was-- the Jews were blamed for everything. The economy was down, the-- if it was hailing, it was the Jews who ordered it. I mean, I'm just saying it as an example. So-- but when it came to those people-- and these people were the ones who after it was carried, all the atrocities, when the time came, the Germans did not need any-- they didn't need to encourage them. They didn't need to worry about it that it won't be carried out as good as they would be doing it, because it was all the orders and all the plans were carried out far better than the Germans could have every dreamed it, without the Germans being involved. Germans were not involved. On the contrary, Germans were more-- more humane than the Hungarians were.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they made remarks like, we want to get rid of the Jews, did they actually say, go back to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other sort of plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the favorite slogan, Jews go back to Palestine. And again, we-- we just took it as-- as-- how do you take people who-- who-- who want to harm you or want to get rid of you? Or-- we took them as they would be jealous because Jews in general were-- were well off. And there were plenty of-- there were [INAUDIBLE] of Jews who were not well off. But only-- only the people, only the Jews who were well off were brought into the sort of the limelight and were made an example, like controlling the economy and-- and-- and taking advantage and-- of the working class and so forth. Same old story like-- like we have it now and like it was a hundred years ago. There's nothing different about it. The only difference was that maybe 100 years ago, there are were isolated pogroms in Russia or anywhere else. And-- and there no mass-- there were no mass murders like it was, for example, in 1944, '45 in Budapest when they lined up people. They dragged them out of the so-called protected houses. They dragged them out and shoved them into the venue. These were the very people-- these were the party people. These were the same people who-- who interrogated in the ghettos. These were the people who-- who put us on the trains. And these were the people who-- who were most upset when they saw Jews coming back after the war. They didn't disappear. They didn't disappear. They-- they were still there. The difference was that they turned Communist instead of Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals at this time as a young man looking towards your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like any-- any young person in those days, my future was more like planned by my family, by my parents, than by myself. Not that I had any objections to it. But I was going to go to the university and become a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] get a degree in-- in-- what do you call it-- in general-- general machinery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of degree is that one? An engineer. Not an engineer like we call-- everybody calls things an engineer today, but there was such a faculty as an engineer. Because-- because they wanted to-- they-- they needed somebody, the family needed somebody, to-- to take over the factory from my grandfather, from my uncle, because I was the next generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And obviously-- actually, electrical engineering was-- was-- was the main and-- the main-- the reason, because-- because that factory was manufacturing all sorts-- all sort of electrical so-called switch boards, so-called fuse boards. And then they started to manufacture all sort of-- it-- it was called condensers, which is also-- we used it for a hundred different purposes. And the other part of the factory was plastic. And in those days, it was called Bakelite. The word still exists, and Bakelite still exists. Only plastic is-- is very different today to what it was at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- I was the oldest as far as the next generation was concerned in-- in the male line who could have taken-- who could take over. Therefore, it was my destiny, planned destiny anyway, to-- to continue and take over. I believe you enjoyed riding your bicycle and did little trips around your district. Can you relate to us any time that you were harassed for-- for being identified as being a Jewish boy on a bicycle or any event?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I can't say that I-- I did because at the time when I was riding the bicycle, [INAUDIBLE] [? it ?] [? could've been-- ?] it was over a long, long period, because we're talking from 1936, 1937 to 1943 maybe, right? And even after the war, one of my hobbies was bicycle. But I-- I really can't say, no. [INAUDIBLE]. No, I wasn't-- I wasn't attacked or any of the kind, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we're finished tape one here. End of tape one.
